description: Student has heard of Pythagoras' theorem but cannot correctly apply it — either skips the squaring step (adds sides), subtracts when should add, forgets the square root, or applies it to non-right triangles.
subject: triangles
diagnosis: The theorem has been taught but not understood. Student knows there is a formula involving three sides but doesn't know the geometric meaning (squares on sides) or the condition (right angle required). Needs geometric proof before procedural practice.
symptoms: Adds sides directly instead of squaring and taking square root
Applies Pythagoras to identify right triangles but gets answer wrong
Cannot identify which side is the hypotenuse in an applied context
intervention_focus: Build Pythagoras from area of squares on sides using grid paper before any formula practice

description: Student confuses multiple angle properties — angle sum (180°), exterior angle theorem, and supplementary angles — applying the wrong rule depending on what comes to mind first.
subject: triangles
diagnosis: Multiple angle rules have been introduced but not organized into a coherent framework. Student applies rules randomly rather than based on the geometric configuration. Needs explicit identification practice for each rule.
symptoms: Uses 360° instead of 180° for triangle angle sum
Applies supplementary rule instead of exterior angle theorem
Gets right answer on some problems but inconsistently across problem types
intervention_focus: Flowchart approach: identify the angle configuration before selecting a rule

description: Student cannot reliably distinguish between altitude, median, and angle bisector in a triangle — often confusing altitude with median or with angle bisector, leading to errors in area and construction problems.
subject: triangles
diagnosis: All three line segments (altitude, median, angle bisector) coincide in equilateral triangles and in the symmetric axis of isosceles triangles. Student has over-generalised from these cases. Needs examples from scalene triangles where all three differ.
symptoms: In area problems, uses a slant side as the height instead of the altitude
Thinks the altitude bisects the base
Thinks the altitude bisects the angle at the vertex
intervention_focus: Scalene triangle constructions where altitude, median, and angle bisector are visually distinct

description: Student cannot correctly classify triangles or use type-specific properties (equilateral, isosceles, scalene) to solve problems — particularly the equal-angles consequence of equal sides.
subject: triangles
diagnosis: Triangle classification is known at the naming level but not at the property-implication level. Student cannot deduce that equal sides → equal angles or use the isosceles base angle property in multi-step problems.
symptoms: Thinks an equilateral triangle can have angles different from 60°
Cannot use isosceles base angle property to find a missing angle
Treats all triangles as scalene when solving angle problems
intervention_focus: If sides are equal, what must be true about angles? Build this implication explicitly
